title: Batch Processing: Managed Batch with Cold Start
description: Batch job execution using managed batch services (AWS Batch, Azure Batch, Google Batch) with on-demand worker provisioning. Workers are provisioned fresh when jobs are submitted — no idle standby pool — resulting in P95 job start times of 2-5 minutes due to VM provisioning, container image pull, and network setup. Most cost-efficient for workloads that tolerate multi-minute delays between submission and execution. Best for nightly ETL, end-of-day reports, periodic data aggregation, and non-urgent background processing where eliminating idle standby costs outweighs startup latency.

cost.costNotes: Cold-start batch has no idle standby costs — pay only for actual job execution time. Most cost-efficient option for infrequent batch jobs. Min ($50): small scheduled jobs (1-2 per day, t3.small, 1hr each ~$1.50/day). Max ($4000): daily heavy ETL on c5.4xlarge instances (6-8hr/day x $0.68/hr x 30 days = $1000-1400) + storage and orchestration.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: On-demand batch provisioning eliminates idle costs. Min ($50): basic scheduled jobs once/day with small instances. Max ($4000): production daily ETL with large c5.4xlarge or r5.2xlarge instances running 6-8 hours daily, S3 storage, and CloudWatch/orchestration costs.
provenance.adoptionCost: Standard managed batch setup — no warm pool complexity. Requires designing for cold start tolerance and idempotent re-runs. Estimate 11-12 developer days at ~$170/day = $2000.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost. AWS Batch, Azure Batch, and Google Batch are usage-based managed services with no upfront licensing. Open-source alternatives (Airflow, Luigi) available.

provides.reasoning: Provides standard batch processing capability via managed batch with on-demand provisioning — cost-optimized for workloads tolerating multi-minute start delays.
Primary use case: nightly ETL, end-of-day processing, and periodic data aggregation where multi-minute start latency is acceptable.
On-demand batch achieves high throughput through horizontal scaling once workers are provisioned; throughput during execution is not limited by startup latency.

requires.reasoning: Managed batch services provide built-in schedulers; cron-based triggers (EventBridge, cron jobs) commonly used for on-demand batch.
Orchestration required to manage job submission, provision monitoring, and coordinate multi-step batch workflows across on-demand workers.
On-demand workers must read from and write to persistent storage as they are terminated after job completion.
Cold-start batch requires retry with sufficient delay to allow new worker provisioning before re-attempting failed jobs.

supports_nfr.reason: Managed batch with cold start is compatible with any auth mechanism; job-level auth handled by IAM roles on provisioned instances.
Managed batch services provide job execution logs and cloud-native audit trails; compatible with both audited and non-audited environments.
Cold-start batch supports tenant isolation through separate job queues with per-tenant IAM roles on provisioned workers.
Managed batch services with cold start can achieve up to 99.99% availability; five-nine availability requires active-active multi-region topology.
Cold-start batch achieves P95 job start of 2-5 minutes (120-300s). Specs requiring <5 min P95 are at the lower bound; pre-provisioned warm pools are more reliable for sub-5-minute requirements.
P99 job start for cold-start batch is 5-10 minutes (300-600 seconds), accounting for slow container pulls, resource contention, and provisioning delays under load.
Cold-start batch RTO is 15-30 minutes: failure detection + re-queuing + fresh worker provisioning (2-5 min) + re-processing from checkpoint.
RPO of 5+ minutes for cold-start batch; data arriving between batch windows is at risk until next scheduled run or re-queue.
Cold-start managed batch handles up to 360M jobs/hour once provisioned; throughput during execution matches warm pool performance.
At <10 jobs/hour, cold-start provisioning overhead (2-5 min per job) dominates execution time. Pre-provisioned infrastructure or serverless functions are more efficient.
Managed cold-start batch is compatible with PII workloads when provisioned instances are configured with appropriate encryption and access controls.
Cold-start batch is compatible with GDPR through proper data handling, deletion capabilities, and ephemeral compute environments.
Cold-start batch supports HIPAA compliance with encryption at rest/transit and BAA coverage for managed services.
Cold-start batch is compatible with CCPA when batch jobs support consumer data deletion within required timeframes.
Cold-start batch supports SOX compliance through managed service job execution logs providing an immutable audit trail.
Cold-start batch is inherently eventually consistent. Read-your-writes requires compensating patterns on top.
Strict durability achievable with idempotent jobs, checkpointing, and transactional writes to durable storage before worker termination.

supports_constraints.reason: Cold-start managed batch is available on all major clouds (AWS Batch, Azure Batch, Google Batch) and on-prem via Kubernetes Jobs.
Cold-start managed batch supports all languages via containerized job images.
Cold-start batch targets scheduled job execution platforms; api platform excluded as batch is not a request-response architecture.
Cold-start batch works with external caches; in-memory caches must be rebuilt on each cold start.
Cold-start batch can be triggered by message queues but primarily uses scheduled triggers or direct job submission.
Cold-start batch supports AI inference in batch jobs; model loading time is included in cold-start overhead.
Cold-start batch supports multi-tenant workloads through separate job queues with per-tenant IAM-scoped on-demand workers.
Pattern requires batch_processing feature flag — this is the cold-start managed batch variant of the batch processing implementation pattern.
Cold-start batch can coordinate distributed transactions within job execution but requires saga patterns for fault tolerance.
Cold-start batch is compatible with lambda architecture; the batch layer handles large-scale historical recomputation.
Cold-start batch can build vector indexes offline in scheduled batch jobs.
Cold-start batch works with document stores for bulk reads and writes in scheduled batch jobs.
Cold-start batch can use key-value stores for job state; connections established fresh on each worker start.
Cold-start batch supports graph analytics and bulk graph mutations in scheduled batch jobs.
Cold-start batch handles time-series aggregation and historical rollup in scheduled nightly or hourly jobs.
Cold-start batch executes bulk OLTP operations during off-peak windows; multi-minute start time is acceptable for nightly batch windows.
Cold-start batch is well-suited for OLAP aggregation jobs running on scheduled windows where query latency matters more than start time.

worker_type.description: Execution environment. Managed-cold-start: managed batch service (AWS Batch, Azure Batch, Google Batch) with on-demand worker provisioning. No idle standby — workers provisioned fresh per job run.

schedule_type.description: How batches are triggered. Cron is most common for cold-start batch with fixed nightly or periodic schedules.

retry_strategy.description: How failed jobs are retried. Exponential_backoff recommended; allow time for fresh worker provisioning before retry.

parallelism.description: Execution model. Partitioned recommended for large cold-start jobs: provision multiple workers in parallel for independent data partitions.

failure_handling.description: What happens when individual records fail. Fail_job recommended for cold-start batch ETL requiring full re-runs from checkpoint on failure.

warn_nfr.reason: Cold-start batch with availability target <95% indicates non-critical workload; simpler cron-based solutions may be more cost-effective than managed batch infrastructure.
warn_nfr.message: data-batch-processing--cold-start: availability target is {actual} (<95%). For non-critical batch jobs, simpler cron-based execution may reduce operational overhead.
